text: Namaskar. Accept greeting from Gastro Institute Kakadev, Kanpur. I am Dr. Brijendra Singh, consultant surgical gastroenterology. I am presenting a video laparoscopic cholecystectomy.

The patient is a forty-eight year male who presented with recurrent episode of right upper abdominal pain for last four months. This pain became severe for last few days. He was clinically examined. He had pulse rate of eighty six per minute. Temperature was normal. His blood pressure was within normal limit. He had tenderness in right upper abdomen. His investigation showed in ultrasound mild hepatomegaly with grade two fatty liver and presence of gallstones, which is called cholelithiasis. His hemogram was normal. Splitlet count was also within normal limit. His liver function test showed a mild rise in SGPT level. His alkaline phosphatase was within normal limit. His other clinical examination and chest X-ray ECG were normal.

Under general anesthesia, the surgeon places the initial 5mm port through the umbilicus and inflates the abdomen with carbon dioxide. Additional ports are placed under direct vision: a 5mm epigastric port, a port in the right lower quadrant, and one in the right flank. The gallbladder is grasped and elevated to expose Calot's triangle.

The surgeon dissects and coagulates the cystic artery within the exposed Calot's triangle. The cystic duct is isolated and looped with an absorbable Vicryl suture. A second suture is placed towards the gallbladder, and the cystic duct is then divided.

The surgeon uses an electrode to dissect the gallbladder from its bed, simultaneously coagulating and cutting the tissue. The area is suctioned dry, and the gallbladder is extracted through the umbilical port, minimizing visible scarring.
